sentences.text: As I--

As I did say, you know, the daily routine of this location, Harka-- terrible name to remember-- it was, if one remembers what was it like, and I think atrocities, how they treated us, were daily routines that you were brutalized physically as you were walking by on those trenches, the location where we had the labor which we had to perform.
We were always reminded of being Jewish.
We were treated like the scum of the world.
And as I remember-- I don't really recall as much of individual scenes of brutality.
Because that was daily routine.
To be shoved and pushed and hit, and-- and-- and-- and-- and blood flowing because they poked you a little bit with the bayonets of the guns.
We accepted that.
The main thing was to survive.
But the general feeling of dehumanization, the way we felt, it is indescribable.
And I am jumping to today-- present day.
Why am I telling that, why am I telling that now?
And I am telling that on the urging of my-- urging of my daughters.
And my wife, who said that you have to do that.
And I want to do it today, between-- just happens to be between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, the Jewish high holidays.
And I owe that, that I am telling this now, to-- to tell what I went through, to tell it for an eyewitness who tells and knows what went on.
And I never done that until now.
As a matter of fact, at my workplace, hardly anybody knew in the hospital that I am a Holocaust survivor.
That terminology-- Holocaust survivor-- you know, it's-- kind of puts everything into one cover.
It's a good term and a bad term as well.
I didn't want that anybody would feel sorry for me.
I talked very little, if anything, about it.
Because I didn't want to people know me and think of me as I am some kind of a strange bird who-- who survived and went through an unbelievable scenes and misery.
And I didn't want anybody to feel sorry for me.
I wanted to be equal, a human being who is doing the work daily, and you fit in and do well in our society.
And I built my presence in daily life and a family and-- and-- enjoyed that I had a family.
And I will now jump time to tell about my parents and what happened when the concentration camp for me was coming to an end.
And how, we will talk about later.
But I just wanted to put in a little philosophy of-- of my feelings, that even now, I don't think I want to tell about scenes of torture, and so on.
Everybody knows.
Whoever paid attention to witnesses' testimonies, that that was definitely part of it.
But dehumanization, and what went in on our mind, why us, and what did we do, is something till today I cannot cope with and understand.
And please ask any questions.
The Russians were coming.
The Russians were coming.
What a-- what a-- a-- a-- an interesting statement to make.
We knew that the Russian front, the war efforts, were happening, and from the Eastern Front, the Russians were approaching our borders, where we were at the Austria-Hungarian border.
Yes.
Yes.
They were dreaming of the days when they're human beings.
They were talking about their families.
They were talking about they wifes, which most of them were separated from.
They were talking about the children, which they didn't know where they were, because they were coming from the Russian front.
My mother could make this food.
My mother could make that food.
Nobody cooked like this.
And so on.
Those were the conversations.
Needs of cigarettes among those who were very much hooked on being a-- habitual cigarette smokers.
They didn't have cigarettes.
Occasionally, some of the guards were throwing the butts of the cigarettes to them and they picked it up from the floor still-- still smoking.
And then they were like seven heaven if they could smoke that.
And dried leaves, dried leaves, dried out and broken up, rolled into some paper foil or dried papers were the way to-- to supplement cigarettes.
Did they have matches?
I don't know how, but somehow some people had even a simple cigarette lighter, which still had some-- some things in it to light up.
And I, myself, started to roll dry leafs because I wanted to be part of the scene.
So when-- when-- when I came back to Budapest after I was freed from the concentration camp, what was next for me?
I became a cigarette smoker.
And I smoked till I was around 40 years old.
I gave up now 28 years ago, on the urging of my children, says you are a doctor.
You should know how bad it does.
And finally, I listened.
I gave up 28 years ago, smoking.
But smoking was, again, something which people missed tremendously and-- and-- and-- they were substituting.
And as a child, I remember that-- that-- what a pleasure it was to them even to inhale and then-- then smoke that awful smelling dry leaf.
Disease, typhoid fever.
People were dying left and right.
And-- and I recall in our barracks the number of people were just taken away.

And the burial society-- burial society-- some very religious Jews, they volunteer, even today, the-- when a Jewish person dies, the Jewish burial society, people who wash the bodies and help with the burial and so on, as far as preparing the body.
There were people who were volunteering their services there to help always with burying people.
And they-- they felt like that they are fulfilling some ancient Jewish law and they did it.
I wasn't among them.
Because my experience after I fainted, I-- I had a-- a tremendous fear of death.
There were some people who were indeed praying every day.
They never lost their beliefs in God.
And there were people whom I observed in our own barrack who-- who were praying daily.
I knew a lot of the prayers by heart.
And I murmured them, hoping that maybe somebody listens up in heaven.
I had some strengths and I had some doubts.
Oh, I was saying morning prayers, which-- which I knew by heart.
And I always negotiated with my father as a child, can I start at [HEBREW] Or-- or can I start at [HEBREW]?
Or can I-- can I start just at the [HEBREW]?
I was-- I was remembering certain of those prayers pretty well.
And-- and I murmured them at times of-- of-- of my own time when I thought that God may be listening to me.
As I said, I had hopes, I had doubts.
I had hopes, I had doubts.
I always remembered my father's blessing that was to me, as strong as anything.
I will survive.
I hope to survive.
I try to survive.
And-- and there were many religious people who-- who were indeed praying more than I did.
But I did my prayers.
I was never publicly showing it.
I just murmured it to myself.
Pardon?
Whatever I remembered.
Whatever I remembered.
And when you say, I negotiated with father, over here, nobody checked on me.
But I remember that the more I was praying according father's wish, that's better.
And-- and then came the time when-- when they evacuated the camp.
Suddenly, when we heard these murmurs from the distance, the commanders, the SS-- there was an SS commander who-- who we nicknamed Z, the black horseman.
We remembered some American movies years back as children, or some older ones, who remembered some Z, the black horseman in some American movies.
So this guy was an SS commander.
And we remember-- I remember German commanders there, the Tudt, T-U-D-T, Tudt organization, who were the architects of the German military.
There was a commander of that unit.
And then the Wehrmacht, which is the German army, was present there.
And then a bunch of SS people who were under the commander, who were then commanding the Arrow Cross people and the [NON-ENGLISH], the Austrian people who volunteered to work in the camps.
And they were not uniformed in the same fashion as the Germans or the Arrow Cross people.
But they were uniformed by armbands of the Volksbund and civilian organizations who were soaked and inundated by Nazi ideas and they volunteered to do the dirty work for them.
And when that situation was going on, the-- the Russians were approaching.
They lined us up.
And they said that the camp will be evacuated.
And they did say that the Jewish chiefs, the [NON-ENGLISH], and these two gentlemen, out of whom I have to mention the name of Kertesz, Mr.
Kertesz, who came to me.
And I said to him, Mr.
Kertesz, I'm not going.
And he says, they are going to kill you right on the spot here.
Anybody who stays behind will be executed.
I said, Mr.
Kertesz, I will try to hide.
And he says, it's your choice.
You have a better chance of surviving if you come along with the other people.
And I said, I don't have the will.
I don't have the strength.
And I have only one hope that I can hide.
It was a decision which just dawned upon me.
And I stuck to it.
So he was very nice to me at that moment.
In the last minute, he reached into his pouch, give me a portion of a loaf of a bread, and he said, well, try to survive on it.
And that's the last time I saw him.
They left and I was staying back with a few number of people in different barracks.
Because as we looked around and we saw some person there, some miserable sight, and that person there.
And in our own barrack, maybe three or four people stayed behind.
They couldn't move.
Their legs were frozen.
They were sick, they were just on the verge of death.
They-- they were exhausted.
And I was hiding.
I was hiding behind some of the barracks structure building up here a little higher and stayed behind.
And that day went by.
I heard from the distance some people moaning and shouting and then silence and gunshots.
And I was pretty sure that that's execution.
And then came the evening and I-- I was desperate for trying to find some food.
My bread didn't last too long.
And in the night as I was walking outside, behind the barrack, the fields, I was hoping I will find some-- some-- any vegetation.
There were fields which the peasants worked on during the summer.
And maybe there is something to be found.
And I found some sugar carrots, piece of sugar carrot, a round sugar carrot, which-- which was wonderful food.
Because we-- we seen those when we were digging trenches sometime.
We found some items such as sugar carrots and then corn left there.
And-- frozen corn, but we ate it and so forth.
And then in the darkness, I noticed somebody around me.
And I saw a woman in a leather coat.
And she was, I figured, probably half-drunk.
And she was just walking around there, between the barracks.
And she reached into her pocket and put in my hand and it was chocolate.
And then in the same hand, there were some metals put into my hand, little coins.
And then she said, Stalin.
Stalin.
I knew who Stalin was.
But what does she have to do with this and what is she talking to me about?
She spoke Russian.
And I didn't understand a word.
She was drunk.
And then, I didn't see her.
I was trying to distance myself and get away and went back to my barrack, and went back to my little haystack area.
And that's the time when I found a tiny prayer book.
It was left there by somebody who was evacuated at the time, or was he alive?
I have no idea.
And that-- that prayer book looked to me like, my god, I am now-- I'm now owning something which is-- which is a connection between me and God.
And I-- I started to pray again.
And next morning, when the light came on, I said all the prayers which my father wanted.
I had the book.
And the bombing and the war came much closer.
I notice smoke with each of the explosions.
And the approaching Russian forces apparently were closing in on the area.
I don't think they were coming for the Jews to liberate them.
That was the war effort.
We just happened to be on the way, I think.
But it was good enough for me.
And I was hoping that I'll be freed.
Just a little distance away, imagine.
You know.
Smoke, gun, artillery fire.
From both directions, from the Germans' side.
And-- and churning tanks on the little road nearby.
And then, suddenly, my barrack gets hit.
And it goes on fire and explode and artillery delivered explodes.
And people who are half-dead there are now bleeding from their wounds.
I am covered with dust and-- and-- and debris, without a scratch.
And I am getting out of the burning barrack, towards the little town, which is just a block or two blocks away.
But we would never dare to go nearby, because that's where the-- the guards were making sure that we stayed on our barrack area of the concentration camp area and not to the near town.
And I am approaching the first house and I-- country houses have cellar doors, to go down and steps.
And I'm going in there, opening the door and going in there, and I see barrels there.
And the barrels somehow gave away to me when I was at the bottom of the steps, they-- they moved.
People apparently were under the barrels and behind the barrels.
And I went behind some barrel and it was semi-dark there.
I saw a person just quietly being there and showing quiet and I was very quiet.
And then in couple of hours, looking through the cracks of the door, we seen some strange uniformed people chasing some German uniformed people with their arms up in the air.
And at that point, one of the barrel moves away, opens up, and the woman who gave me the chocolate piece and coins gets out.
And as I recall her identity as I seen her, she was a very courageous Russian spy who spoke German language and was a woman who was hiding her identity with the German soldiers.
And in her drunkness that night, she not knowing what she's saying, she revealed that she speaks Russian to me.
And that woman immediately directed the Russians down to the cellar.
And the Russians came in with-- with their machine guns.
And it happens that in the cellar were at least 15, 18 people, half of them German soldiers who were hiding and giving up and they wanted to be captured.
Some of them were Arrow Cross people.
And myself, and maybe two other, were Jews like myself, who didn't go away with the evacuation of the camp.
And I was freed.
I was freed.
And-- and the war went on.
And--
No.
No.
I didn't care the German soldiers' name.
And the two other people who were there, they didn't care about me.
Everybody was trying to find out what happened to-- is anybody else in the camp alive, and so on.
And I was trying to find out what happened to Tomas Stern.
I had no idea if he left or stayed behind.
Would you like to ask something?
What did you find out about Tomas Stern?
That he was no more alive.
He was not buried yet.
But he was no more alive.
I-- I-- I-- I saw him.
Dead.
He just died in that so-called medical unit.
And they buried him.
And then I was walking away and, and saddened.
And my aim was now how to get back-- how to get back to Budapest.
And as I was walking-- Russian soldiers, they didn't have special sympathy for us.
There were a couple of those souls, like half-dead people wandering around.
And this soldier, which-- whom I happened to find myself face-to-face, Russian soldier.
He was drunk as can be.
And he was just moving around with his gun and the bayonet and he was just fighting the war.
Anybody human being alive next to him, he was ready to kill.
So he lifted up his bayonet and the gun-- I mean the-- rifle, and was shoving down against me.
I moved.
And he had made a big slash on my canteen, which was hanging on some kind of a little string or belt, or whatever I had.
I was almost given my final blow by him.
Nevertheless, I moved away.
And then comes the story of how did I return to Budapest.
That was late, late, at night.
And I waited for the next morning.
And I slept in the ruins of that barrack where I was, and talked to some of the people who were injured and survived, and some Russian people and town people then came out.
And they did try to help them, and so on.
And next morning, I took off with some six or eight people in the road, knowing the Hungarian geography countryside, towards the town of Sopron.
And then further to go towards Papa, P-A-P-A.
And I couldn't keep up [INAUDIBLE] this six or eight people.
They were moving faster than I did.
So I said, I am staying behind.
I was just dragging myself along.
And they were moving on.
I lost sight of them on the highway.
They were-- I was all by myself.
And in another hour or so, you know-- I don't know where one gets the strength when one is free, but one is moving on to move towards freedom, to-- to-- to get somewhere.
And then then I see couple of Russian tanks on the highway, moving in my direction.
And they slow down as I was dragging myself along the highway.
And the guy in the tank points at me and he say, [NON-ENGLISH] I didn't know what it means.
But [NON-ENGLISH].
The I figured out and I said, Jew.
Jude.
In German, Jude.
And he lifts me up on the tank and takes me along.
The Russian retreat.
The Germans were closing in on the highway.
And I retreated back to Harka, a three- or four-kilometer distance which I managed to walk on my own, on a Russian tank.
That section of that attack was over.
And a Russian person who spoke Yiddish, which I didn't speak, found out about me.
And-- and I tried to converse with him.
And I was telling that Papa, Papa.
That's where I want to go.
I know that that's where-- a big town.
Maybe there is some train.
Budapest.
Papa.
And he said, Papa.
Mama.
He misunderstood my meaning, but I-- I motioned that yes, that's [INAUDIBLE].
And-- and then, he just kind of hold onto me, not with his arms around me, but hold onto me.
And I followed him.
And he took me to a peasant house.
And-- and he talked words which now I remember like [NON-ENGLISH], which means bread.
And he was pointing to the peasants that they should feed me.
And I wanted egg.
Egg.
To eat egg.
And I told the peasants that I want egg.
And he understood my request.
But the peasants didn't come forward with eggs.
So he took out his machine gun and in the courtyard, he started to shoot all the chickens.
Then they said stop.
And there was egg.
And I had my plate of eggs.
Next day, he put me on a truck with some Russians who were going backwards, towards the town of next one and next one, on through.
They arrived to a place where there was some-- some storage area for Russian supplies and food.
We-- change tape.
And we change tape and I continue.
